fragmentation and degradation in the past decades.
The areas surveyed experienced some of the highest 
rural population densities in the world ranging from 400 to 1,300 inhabitants per km2.
This is in line with the 
situation in the project areas with around 430 km2 inhabitants.

Zero hunger.summary: No supporting evidence was found to determine the percentage change in Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) stock in agricultural land due to sustainable soil management practices implemented in the reporting period. The context mentions the adoption of SALM practices such as mulching, reduced tillage, agroforestry, cover crops, and soil and water conservation, but does not provide specific data on the impact of these practices on SOC stock. The baseline SOC stock in agricultural land before the implementation of sustainable soil management practices and the SOC stock in agricultural land after the implementation of sustainable soil management practices in the reporting period are also not provided. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Presenting the maps is not con- sidered sufficient evidence that the planting areas are not on wetlands and/or organic soils.  AR 08: Section A.2 indicates on page 10 “Project has single GPS location for each farm registered  under project. Using Extract values from raster tool in GIS software value of forest or non-forest was  assigned to each farm location.” It is understood that the PD used the center points of the farms to  determine if there  was forest in the  past 10  years within the farms. However, this approach  is not  conservative. If the exact shape of the boundaries of each farm is not known, the PD should use a  conservative approach and apply a circular buffer around the centerpoints to account for the actual  area of each farm.  he  snapshots  from  a  section  of  the  participating  farms  illustrate  the  principles  described  above  (green areas are forest in 2006 as per the data provided by the PD). The second snapshot shows  how center points that do not overlap with forest in the first snapshot, can actually fall on forest when  a circular area boundary is applied. In the absence of more spatially detailed data on the boundaries  of the farms, the PD is requested to take a conservative approach and discard from the planting area  any potential lands that could be considered forest. The CAR remains open.

A.4. Location of the project: A.5.  Technologies  and/or measures | : The project is implemented by Vi Agroforestry, an NGO with a long  track  record  of  implementation  of  livelihoods  improvement  pro- jects/ViWeb/.  It aims to reach out to 30.000  farmers out of which 15.000 being  engaged in dairy farming.  Gender equality is seriously taken into account – to ensure the pro- ject  improve  gender  relations  and  benefits  to  both  women  and  men/ViAGM, ViAGM/.   It aims to facilitate the up-setting/revitalize self-help facilities such  as farmers groups, farmers cooperatives and dairy hubs.  It  promotes  climate-smart  sustainable  agricultural  land  manage- ment practices/ViAAP,ViASTM/ (SALM) and improved livestock feeding  and husbandry practices/ViADP,ViADT/ such as:  Mulching    Composting    Soil and water conservation structures,    Soil fertility tree planting    Long term tree planting (inter-planting, boundary planting, fruit    orchards, trees in crop and livestock agroforestry systems)  Improving dairy production (improved feeding, housing, water-   ing,  breeding,  disease  control,  better  collection,  storage  and  use practices)  For the implementation a three-level system was established with  ViAgroforestry as project and field coordinators, farmer groups and  cooperatives as facilitators in charge of e.g. detecting the farm sta- tus, monitoring of practices, transferring collected data into the pro- ject’s MIS systems (web-based) /MIS/ etc. and the farmers as active  beneficiary  of the project. | : OK

Specifically for this project the following SDGs  have been integrated into the KPIs of the project defined in  the  contract  with  Livelihoods  and  subject  to  performance  monitoring and impact assessment:   SDG  1:  No  Poverty  &  SDG  2:  Zero  Hunger  →  Improve  knowledge, skills and income of 30,000 participating farmers  to  reach  better  life  quality;  increase  family  health  and  nutritional status of 30,000 participating farmers;  SDG 5: Gender Equality→  Increase  women participation to  the farmer’s organization  SDG 13: Climate Action→ Generate mitigation benefits from  reduced  dairy emissions: At least 35,100 hectares implemented within a total project  area of 292,390 ha; The project typically follows the logic of  grouped smallholder project under the LUF GSF guidance -  since  smallholder farmers are  recruited in either existing or  newly  formed  farmer  groups  within  a  larger  project  region  (292,390  ha)  defined  by  the  Kenyan  administrative  boundaries  of  sub-county,  ward  and  location. A.4. Location of the project: A.5.  Technologies  and/or measures | : The project is implemented by Vi Agroforestry, an NGO with a long  track  record  of  implementation  of  livelihoods  improvement  pro- jects/ViWeb/.  It aims to reach out to 30.000  farmers out of which 15.000 being  engaged in dairy farming.  Gender equality is seriously taken into account – to ensure the pro- ject  improve  gender  relations  and  benefits  to  both  women  and  men/ViAGM, ViAGM/.   It aims to facilitate the up-setting/revitalize self-help facilities such  as farmers groups, farmers cooperatives and dairy hubs.  It  promotes  climate-smart  sustainable  agricultural  land  manage- ment practices/ViAAP,ViASTM/ (SALM) and improved livestock feeding  and husbandry practices/ViADP,ViADT/ such as:  Mulching    Composting    Soil and water conservation structures,    Soil fertility tree planting    Long term tree planting (inter-planting, boundary planting, fruit    orchards, trees in crop and livestock agroforestry systems)  Improving dairy production (improved feeding, housing, water-   ing,  breeding,  disease  control,  better  collection,  storage  and  use practices)  For the implementation a three-level system was established with  ViAgroforestry as project and field coordinators, farmer groups and  cooperatives as facilitators in charge of e.g. detecting the farm sta- tus, monitoring of practices, transferring collected data into the pro- ject’s MIS systems (web-based) /MIS/ etc. and the farmers as active  beneficiary  of the project. | : OK

3. Good health and well being.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, 'Area under reduced/avoided open burning of biomass, crop residue'. Although the project promotes sustainable land management practices and alternative technologies to avoid open biomass/crop residue burning, the total land area where these practices are implemented is not explicitly stated. The baseline area under open burning of biomass/crop residue before the implementation of sustainable land management practices is also not explicitly stated. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative only, and the score is 1.

A.4. Location of the project: A.5.  Technologies  and/or measures | : The project is implemented by Vi Agroforestry, an NGO with a long  track  record  of  implementation  of  livelihoods  improvement  pro- jects/ViWeb/.  It aims to reach out to 30.000  farmers out of which 15.000 being  engaged in dairy farming.  Gender equality is seriously taken into account – to ensure the pro- ject  improve  gender  relations  and  benefits  to  both  women  and  men/ViAGM, ViAGM/.   It aims to facilitate the up-setting/revitalize self-help facilities such  as farmers groups, farmers cooperatives and dairy hubs.  It  promotes  climate-smart  sustainable  agricultural  land  manage- ment practices/ViAAP,ViASTM/ (SALM) and improved livestock feeding  and husbandry practices/ViADP,ViADT/ such as:  Mulching    Composting    Soil and water conservation structures,    Soil fertility tree planting    Long term tree planting (inter-planting, boundary planting, fruit    orchards, trees in crop and livestock agroforestry systems)  Improving dairy production (improved feeding, housing, water-   ing,  breeding,  disease  control,  better  collection,  storage  and  use practices)  For the implementation a three-level system was established with  ViAgroforestry as project and field coordinators, farmer groups and  cooperatives as facilitators in charge of e.g. detecting the farm sta- tus, monitoring of practices, transferring collected data into the pro- ject’s MIS systems (web-based) /MIS/ etc. and the farmers as active  beneficiary  of the project. | : OK

3. Good health and well being.indicator: Area under reduced/avoided open burning of biomass, crop residue

3. Good health and well being.summary: The evidence from the Intermediate Answers satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement. The project has shown a significant reduction in fire occurrence to a scale of 0 to 0.1%/KACP/ (Document: GS6588_2021-09-08_GS+FVal_MtElgon_clean%281%29.pdf, Page: 50), which directly measures the indicator variable. 5. Gender equality.summary: The provided context does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures the proportion of women in managerial positions. The context mentions various numbers of farmers, community facilitators, and technical officers, but does not specify their roles or positions. The baseline survey in 2015 showed 62% of women participating in farmer organizations, and the FAR review in 2022 states that the percentage of women in the group has increased by 4%, from 63% to the current 67%. However, these statistics do not measure the proportion of women in managerial positions. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Crediting period of project  
The crediting period has been set as per  the methodology:: 
• 
Activities under Gold Standard Agriculture Smallholder Dairy Methodology have 7 years crediting 
period, which can be extended twice.
Therefore, the project crediting period is 7 years, with at least 
one renewal of another 7 years.

Due to project activities is assumed that the milk production  per cow is going to increase over the calculation period. 5. Gender equality.summary: No explicit quantitative data is found to directly measure the number of unique individual female managers employed by the organization. The context provides information on the percentage of women participating in farmer organizations, which is reported to be 62% and 67% in the baseline survey and current monitoring, respectively. However, these statistics do not provide information on female managers. The total number of female employees in the organization is unknown, as only 13,945 female farmers are registered in the MIS. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from the Monitoring Report (T-PerfCert_V1.1-Mt+Elgon+Project_Monitoring+Report_ver4_with+reviews+and+deviation+-+VF.pdf, Page: 41, 77) mentions the project's promotion of Sustainable Agriculture Land Management (SALM) practices, including agroforestry, which aims to recharge groundwater through increased infiltration and reduce runoff due to tree planting. However, the project's impact on water bodies and erosion is not explicitly quantified. The evidence does not directly measure the water retention capacity, as it only mentions the adoption of SALM practices and the number of long-term trees per farmer, which is not a direct measure of water retention capacity. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

Validation Report:: Summary of  Validation Opinion: | Report No.  Rev. No.: Livelihoods Venture has commissioned the TÜV NORD JI/CDM Certification Program  (CP) to validate and verify the project: “Livelihoods Mount Elgon Project” with regard to  the relevant requirements of the Gold Standard Land-use & Forests Activity (A/R) Re- quirements  version  1.1,  host  country  criteria,  as  well  as  criteria  given  to  provide  for  consistent project operations, monitoring and reporting.  The project activity “Livelihoods Mount Elgon Project”, including at least 35,100 ha of  eligible farmer land, conforms to the performance certification criteria set in the docu- ment GS4GG Agriculture Smallholder Dairy Methodology v1.0.  The assessment team reviewed the estimation of the projected emission removals. It  can be confirmed that the indicated amount of emission removals of  386,358 t CO2e  over the crediting period of 7 years, resulting in a calculated annual average of 55,194t  CO2e  represent  a  reasonable  estimation  using  the assumptions  given  by  the project  documents.  In summary, it is TÜV NORD JI/CDM Certification Program (CP)’s opinion that the pro- ject meets all relevant GS4GG Land-Use & Forests Activity (A/R) Requirements, v1.1  and GS4GG Agriculture Smallholder Dairy Methodology v1.0. TÜV NORD JI/CDM Cer- tification Program (CP) thus requests the registration of the project as a Gold Standard  Afforestation/Reforestation (A/R) project activity. | Date of 1st  issue::  | Date of this rev.:  | :

beneficiaries – the 30,000 smallholder farmers, at least 15,000 dairy farmers and the farmer

negative land use change.

spillover effects also to other lands which increase the potential to recover.

the Kenya Agricultural Carbon Project, a VCS registered project adjacent to this project which is also 
implemented since 2009 under the same conditions and with the same training and extension 
approach by Vi (except the dairy component).

The  project-promotes  a  holistic  approach  with  agroforestry  practices  that  make  use  of  tree  species  that  are  indigenous  and  adapted  to  the  local  climate  variabilities  and  changes.  The  sustainable  agriculture  land  management  practices  such  as  im- proved  crop  varieties,  change  of  crop  types,  pest  or  drought  tolerant  crops  and  intercropping  are  promoted  to  cope  with  extreme weather events. Other agronomic  practices  to  mitigate  extreme  weather  in- clude contour strip cropping, use of cover  crops and green manure, fodder banks and  integrated pest management. Agroforestry  also gives farmers more products from the  farm.  Farmers  are  planting  fodder  and  have been trained on fodder storage.   The  positive  effects  on  these  measures  have  been  observed  in  the  KACP,  where  crop yields continue to increase and stabi- lize on higher levels compared to the base- line yields even when there have been ex- treme water events such as drought/KACP/. | : Pre- sent  Score | : Cor- rected  Score | : OK

Calculation  of net benefits as dif- ference  of  baseline  and project values or  direct  calculation  for  each SDG outcome | : Emission reductions (ER) are calculated as baseline emission in- tensity (BE) intensity minus project emission intensity (PE)  Net emission reductions are calculated as project emission reduc- tions minus leakage due to land use change.   In order to clean the data collected for baseline emissions intensity  as well as project emission intensity outlier were checked in the  monitored dataset used for the calculations. As the standard devi- ation could not be used as the data did not follow a normal distri- bution, the interquartile range (IQR) was used to identify outliers  by multiplying the IQR by 1.5 subtracting the result from the first  Quartile Q1, and adding to the third Quartile, Q3. Any milk yield  value outside of these two resulting values was considered an out- lier.   Lower Outliers < Q1-IQR*1.5  Upper Outliers > Q3+IQR*1.5  All calculation steps could be followed in fully traceable excel-files  provided. AR 08: Section A.2 indicates on page 10 “Project has single GPS location for each farm registered  under project. Using Extract values from raster tool in GIS software value of forest or non-forest was  assigned to each farm location.” It is understood that the PD used the center points of the farms to  determine if there  was forest in the  past 10  years within the farms. However, this approach  is not  conservative. If the exact shape of the boundaries of each farm is not known, the PD should use a  conservative approach and apply a circular buffer around the centerpoints to account for the actual  area of each farm.  he  snapshots  from  a  section  of  the  participating  farms  illustrate  the  principles  described  above  (green areas are forest in 2006 as per the data provided by the PD). The second snapshot shows  how center points that do not overlap with forest in the first snapshot, can actually fall on forest when  a circular area boundary is applied. In the absence of more spatially detailed data on the boundaries  of the farms, the PD is requested to take a conservative approach and discard from the planting area  any potential lands that could be considered forest. The CAR remains open.

The  project-promotes  a  holistic  approach  with  agroforestry  practices  that  make  use  of  tree  species  that  are  indigenous  and  adapted  to  the  local  climate  variabilities  and  changes.  The  sustainable  agriculture  land  management  practices  such  as  im- proved  crop  varieties,  change  of  crop  types,  pest  or  drought  tolerant  crops  and  intercropping  are  promoted  to  cope  with  extreme weather events. Other agronomic  practices  to  mitigate  extreme  weather  in- clude contour strip cropping, use of cover  crops and green manure, fodder banks and  integrated pest management. Agroforestry  also gives farmers more products from the  farm.  Farmers  are  planting  fodder  and  have been trained on fodder storage.   The  positive  effects  on  these  measures  have  been  observed  in  the  KACP,  where  crop yields continue to increase and stabi- lize on higher levels compared to the base- line yields even when there have been ex- treme water events such as drought/KACP/. | : Pre- sent  Score | : Cor- rected  Score | : OK

Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

Technologies  and/or measures | : The project is implemented by Vi Agroforestry, an NGO with a long  track  record  of  implementation  of  livelihoods  improvement  pro- jects/ViWeb/.  It aims to reach out to 30.000  farmers out of which 15.000 being  engaged in dairy farming.  Gender equality is seriously taken into account – to ensure the pro- ject  improve  gender  relations  and  benefits  to  both  women  and  men/ViAGM, ViAGM/.   It aims to facilitate the up-setting/revitalize self-help facilities such  as farmers groups, farmers cooperatives and dairy hubs.  It  promotes  climate-smart  sustainable  agricultural  land  manage- ment practices/ViAAP,ViASTM/ (SALM) and improved livestock feeding  and husbandry practices/ViADP,ViADT/ such as:  Mulching    Composting    Soil and water conservation structures,    Soil fertility tree planting    Long term tree planting (inter-planting, boundary planting, fruit    orchards, trees in crop and livestock agroforestry systems)  Improving dairy production (improved feeding, housing, water-   ing,  breeding,  disease  control,  better  collection,  storage  and  use practices)  For the implementation a three-level system was established with  ViAgroforestry as project and field coordinators, farmer groups and  cooperatives as facilitators in charge of e.g. detecting the farm sta- tus, monitoring of practices, transferring collected data into the pro- ject’s MIS systems (web-based) /MIS/ etc. and the farmers as active  beneficiary  of the project. | : OK

Risk  of  change  to  the  Project  activities  during Project Certification Period:: Main social impacts (risks and benefits) | There  is  no  risk  of  change  to  the  project  activities  during  Project  Certification  Period  since  project  activities  are  farmer-selected and mutually agreed upon, then specified in  the  contractual  agreement  signed  between  the  Project  Implementer and participating farmers.: E The  project  is  building  farmers’  capacities  through  training  and technical support  in  improved livestock husbandry and  sustainable  agricultural  land  management.  An  improved  dairy value chain is being developed with the establishment  and strengthening of dairy cooperatives and dairy hubs. The  milk is sourced by Brookside dairy also investing in the dairy  infrastructure  development.  These  activities  will  result  in  increased  capacities  of  the  farmers  to  manage  their  farms  sustainably, and generate increased benefits – food, income  from  dairy,  and  nutrition.  The  social  and  economic  KPIs  defined  in  the  contract  between  Livelihoods  and  Vi  Agroforestry for the project include:  Social goals   (i)  Improve  knowledge,  skills  and  income  of  30,000  participating farmers to reach better life quality;   (ii)  Increase  family  health  and  nutritional  status  of  30,000  participating farmers;  (iii)  Establish  a  strong  and  professional  institution  for  business and social empowerment; and  (iv)  Increase  women  participation  to  the  farmer’s  organization.  conomic goals   (i)  Improve  quality  and  increase  capacity  of  average  milk  production; and   (ii)  Deliver  value  across  carbon  credits  involving  30,000  farmers,  of  which  15,000  farmers  are  involved  in  dairy  production.
